description: Why 0.1 + 0.2 does not equal exactly 0.3 in Python
initial_misconception: Computers should handle simple decimal numbers exactly, like a calculator.
initial_student_statement: I don't understand why Python says 0.1 + 0.2 is 0.30000000000000004. That seems like a bug. My calculator handles this just fine.
correct_understanding: Floating point numbers are stored as binary fractions (IEEE 754), so most decimal fractions cannot be represented exactly, causing tiny rounding errors that accumulate during arithmetic.
good_question_templates: How do you think computers store numbers internally?
What would it mean to represent 0.1 in base 2?
Have you ever seen a number that can't be expressed exactly as a fraction?
What happens when you add two approximations together?
Can you think of a number in base 10 that repeats forever?
What does 1/3 look like in decimal form?
If a number can't be stored exactly, what happens to the tiny leftover?

confused: I don't get it. Computers are really powerful, so why can't they just store 0.1 correctly?
But 0.1 is such a simple number... why would a computer have trouble with it?
uncertain: Hmm, so computers use something different than regular numbers? I'm not sure I follow.
Wait, are you saying computers don't use normal decimal digits inside?
starting_to_see: Oh... so like how 1/3 is 0.333 forever in decimal? Some numbers repeat in binary too?
So if 0.1 is something like 0.000110011... forever in binary, the computer has to cut it off?
almost_there: So 0.1 is already slightly wrong when stored, and 0.2 is also slightly wrong... and when you add them the errors combine?
I think I see — both numbers are approximations, so their sum inherits both approximation errors.
understood: Oh! So 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3 because BOTH 0.1 and 0.2 are already slightly off due to binary representation, and those tiny errors add up to something visible!
I get it now — it's not a bug, it's a fundamental limitation of representing base-10 fractions in base-2. The error was always there, we just couldn't see it with one number.
